summary:
The "No Visas for Anti-Semitic Students Act" (H.R. 2866) directs the Secretary of State to revoke or deny student visas to foreign nationals who engage in antisemitic conduct.

Under Section 2, the Secretary of State must revoke or deny a visa issued under subparagraph (F) or (M) of section 101(a)(15) of the Immigration and Nationality Act—categories covering academic and vocational students—for any alien who, on or after the bill's enactment date, both (1) engages in "prohibited antisemitic conduct" and (2) is determined by the Secretary to pose potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences for the United States under section 237(a)(4)(C)(i) of the Act.

The bill defines "antisemitism" using the definition adopted by the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance on May 26, 2016, as adopted by the State Department, including its "contemporary examples of antisemitism." It defines "prohibited antisemitic conduct" as either (A) committing a physical act of violence, vandalism, or harassment targeting a Jewish individual, their property, a community institution, or a religious facility, with intent to intimidate or harm based on Jewish identity or affiliation; or (B) providing material support—including funding, organizing, or inciting—to a person knowing they intended to use it to commit such an act.

Section 3 amends section 214 of the Immigration and Nationality Act by adding a new subsection (s). This provision states that an alien may not be granted or maintain (F) or (M) nonimmigrant status if the alien engages in prohibited antisemitic conduct and the Secretary determines such conduct would have potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences. It further requires the Secretary to revoke the visa of an alien already holding such status upon making these determinations. The new subsection incorporates the same definitions of "antisemitism" and "prohibited antisemitic conduct."

The bill's provisions apply to conduct occurring on or after the date of enactment. It was introduced on April 10, 2025, and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
